1:
πρὸ δὲ τῆς ἑορτῆς τοῦ πάσχα — "before the festival of the Passover." The temporal marker sets this chapter earlier than the Synoptics' Last Supper timeline; in John the crucifixion occurs on Nisan 14 (the day of Passover preparation) rather than Nisan 15, making Jesus the Passover lamb killed when the lambs are slaughtered.

ἀγαπήσας τοὺς ἰδίους τοὺς ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ, εἰς τέλος ἠγάπησεν αὐτούς — "having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end." Agapaō (G25) appears as a participle (prior love) then aorist (the definitive act). Eis telos (G5056) carries both temporal sense ("to the end, until the last moment") and qualitative sense ("completely, utterly") — the ambiguity is intentional; both are true of what follows in these chapters and at the cross.
2:
δείπνου γινομένου — "during supper being held/underway," present participle of ginomai — the meal is in progress, not concluded (contrast earlier translations that read "when supper was ended").

τοῦ διαβόλου ἤδη βεβληκότος εἰς τὴν καρδίαν — "the devil having already cast into the heart," perfect active participle of ballō (G906, to throw/cast). The perfect marks the devil's prompting as a completed instigation: Judas's heart is already set before the meal's action begins. Diabolos (G1228, the accuser/slanderer) is used here rather than Satanas (John 13:27), noting both terms converge on the same figure.
3:
εἰδὼς ὅτι πάντα ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ ὁ πατήρ εἰς τὰς χεῖρας — "knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands," perfect of didōmi. The cosmic frame is essential: Jesus washes feet not in submission to the disciples but from a position of total authority. The humility is voluntary, not imposed.

ἀπὸ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθεν καὶ πρὸς τὸν θεὸν ὑπάγει — "from God he came out and to God he is going," aorist of exerchomai + present of hypagō. The framing of Jesus' existence between two divine termini — departure from God and return to God — is the theological ground for the shocking act that follows. He who spans eternity stoops to wash feet.
4:
τίθησιν τὰ ἱμάτια — "lays down his garments," present of tithēmi (G5087). The same verb is used for laying down one's life (John 10:11, 15, 17, 18; John 13:37–38). The Evangelist chooses tithēmi for the garment-laying precisely to anticipate the cross-language.

λέντιον διέζωσεν ἑαυτόν — "wrapped a linen cloth around himself," aorist of diazōnnymi (G1241). Lention (G3012) is a Latin loanword (linteum) — a linen towel, the garb of a household slave. The detail makes the status-inversion explicit.
5:
εἶτα βάλλει ὕδωρ εἰς τὸν νιπτῆρα — "then he pours water into the basin," present tense continuing the vivid narration. Niptēr (G3537, a washbasin) appears only here in NT.

ἤρξατο νίπτειν τοὺς πόδας τῶν μαθητῶν — "he began to wash the feet of the disciples," niptō (G3538, to wash a body part). The distinction from louō (G3068, full bathing) matters for John 13:9–10. Foot-washing was a task for the lowest-ranked household slave, not for a rabbi — this is the crux of Peter's objection.
6: κύριε, σύ μου νίπτεις τοὺς πόδας — "Lord, are you washing my feet?" The word order in Greek puts su (you) immediately after the address, making the contrast emphatic: You — of all people — wash my feet? Peter's objection registers the role-reversal as an impropriety, not merely a surprise.
7:
ὃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ σὺ οὐκ οἶδας ἄρτι — "what I am doing you do not understand now," present of oida (G1492). Arti (G737, now/at this moment) — current incomprehension is normal; understanding will come. The word oida in John often denotes deeper comprehension, not surface knowledge; Peter's deficiency is theological, not informational.

γνώσῃ δὲ μετὰ ταῦτα — "but you will know after these things," future of ginōskō. The post-resurrection understanding promised to the disciples (cf. John 12:16) is anticipated here.
8:
οὐ μὴ νίψῃς μου τοὺς πόδας εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "you shall by no means wash my feet forever," strong negation (ou mē + aorist subjunctive). Eis ton aiōna ("into the age") is the standard emphatic expression for "never" in a permanent sense. Peter's refusal is categorical.

οὐκ ἔχεις μέρος μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ — "you have no part with me," present of echō. Meros (G3313, part/share) carries the sense of participation and belonging — an inheritance or allotment term (cf. Joshua 14:4 LXX; Revelation 20:6). Exclusion from meros is exclusion from the shared life Jesus offers.
9: μὴ μόνον τοὺς πόδας ἀλλὰ καὶ τὰς χεῖρας καὶ τὴν κεφαλήν — "not only my feet but also my hands and head." Peter's characteristic overcorrection swings from categorical refusal to total submission. The three body parts (feet, hands, head) may reflect a complete-cleansing idiom, or simply Petrine impulsiveness.
10:
ὁ λελουμένος οὐ χρείαν ἔχει εἰ μὴ τοὺς πόδας νίψασθαι — "the one who has bathed does not need anything except to wash his feet," perfect passive participle of louō (G3068, full immersion bathing). The distinction between louō (whole-body bath, once) and niptō (partial washing, ongoing) is deliberate. A person who has bathed needs only foot-cleaning after walking — the metaphor suggests that the disciples have already received comprehensive cleansing (discipleship with Jesus) and need only the ongoing foot-washing (continued service and humility).

καὶ ὑμεῖς καθαροί ἐστε, ἀλλ᾽ οὐχὶ πάντες — "and you are clean, but not all of you," katharos (G2513, clean/pure). The qualification "not all" prepares for John 13:11's identification of Judas, and anticipates the moral dimension of the cleansing metaphor.
11: ᾔδει γὰρ τὸν παραδιδόντα αὐτόν — "for he knew the one who was betraying him," pluperfect of oida — "had known." The parenthetical narrator comment reveals that Jesus' "not all" in John 13:10 was deliberate and knowing, not uncertain.
12:
ἔλαβεν τὰ ἱμάτια αὐτοῦ καὶ ἀνέπεσεν πάλιν — "he took his garments and reclined again," aorists marking the resumption of normal table position. The deliberateness of putting garments back on — completing the re-dressing before asking the interpretive question — underlines that this is a structured teaching act, not spontaneous.

γινώσκετε τί πεποίηκα ὑμῖν — "do you understand what I have done for you?" present of ginōskō + perfect of poieō. The completed act now awaits interpretation — the question is pedagogical, inviting reflection before explanation.
13:
ὁ διδάσκαλος καὶ ὁ κύριος — "the Teacher and the Lord," both with the definite article. These titles represent the two poles of Jesus' acknowledged authority: didaskalos (G1320, rabbi/teacher) is the common address used by disciples; kyrios (G2962, Lord/master) acknowledges sovereign authority. Jesus affirms both simultaneously.

καλῶς λέγετε, εἰμὶ γάρ — "you speak rightly, for I am," present of eimi. The affirmation is unqualified — Jesus does not diminish the titles but draws on their combined authority for the foot-washing argument.
14:
εἰ οὖν ἐγὼ ἔνιψα ὑμῶν τοὺς πόδας ὁ κύριος καὶ ὁ διδάσκαλος — "if then I, the Lord and the Teacher, washed your feet," aorist of niptō. The conditional is real (first class implied): he did wash them. The argument from the greater to the lesser (a fortiori) is explicit — if the one of higher rank performed the act, those of lower rank are obligated.

ὀφείλετε καὶ ὑμεῖς ἀλλήλων νίπτειν τοὺς πόδας — "you also ought to wash one another's feet," present infinitive of niptō indicating an ongoing obligation. Opheilō (G3784, to owe/ought) is not mere suggestion but moral obligation derived from the enacted example.
15:
ὑπόδειγμα γὰρ δέδωκα ὑμῖν — "for I have given you an example," perfect of didōmi. Hypodeigma (G5261, example/pattern/model) appears here for the first time in John — this is the interpretive key Jesus provides. Not a one-time rite but a repeatable pattern of servant-action among equals.

ἵνα καθὼς ἐγὼ ἐποίησα ὑμῖν, καὶ ὑμεῖς ποιῆτε — "so that as I did for you, you also might do," aorist subjunctive of poieō. The kathōs (just as) establishes correspondence of kind, not merely of external form — the quality of self-giving is the model, not the literal act only.
16:
οὐκ ἔστιν δοῦλος μείζων τοῦ κυρίου αὐτοῦ — "a servant is not greater than his master," present of eimi. The principle appears also at John 15:20; the axiom is well-attested in Jewish wisdom literature. Applied here: if the master washed feet, the servant has no grounds to refuse to do the same.

οὐδὲ ἀπόστολος μείζων τοῦ πέμψαντος αὐτόν — "nor is a messenger/apostle greater than the one who sent him." Apostolos (G652) appears only here in John — the only occurrence in the entire Gospel. The word means "sent one" (from apostellō, G649) — the parallel with pempō (sent) is intentional: Jesus is himself the one sent by the Father (passim), and the disciples are sent by him.
17: εἰ ταῦτα οἴδατε, μακάριοί ἐστε ἐὰν ποιῆτε αὐτά — "if you know these things, you are blessed if you do them." Two conditionals bracket the promise: knowing (oida) and doing (poieō). Makarios (G3107, blessed/happy) is a beatitude-form — the only such form in John's Gospel. Knowing without doing produces no blessing; action in conformity with knowledge does.
18:
οὐ περὶ πάντων ὑμῶν λέγω — "I am not speaking about all of you," present of legō. The assurance of John 13:17 is explicitly not addressed to Judas — the exception is stated before the citation so readers understand Jesus' blessing has not missed the betrayal.

ὁ τρώγων μου τὸν ἄρτον ἐπῆρεν ἐπ᾽ ἐμὲ τὴν πτέρναν αὐτοῦ — Psalms 41:9 (LXX John 40:10) citation. Trōgō (G5176, to gnaw or crunch) is a more visceral eating verb than esthiō (G2068) — the LXX uses esthiō but John's version upgrades to trōgō, the same verb Jesus used for eating his flesh at John 6:54–58. The betrayal occurs in the context of shared bread, which was the ultimate expression of covenant solidarity in the ancient world.
19: ἵνα πιστεύσητε ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι — "so that you may believe that I am," aorist subjunctive of pisteuō. ἐγώ εἰμι without predicate is the Johannine absolute divine self-identification (cf. John 8:24, 28, 58), carrying the register of the divine name from Exodus 3:14 (LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν) and Isaiah 43:10 (LXX: ἐγώ εἰμι). Foreknowledge of the betrayal, announced before it happens, becomes evidence for the disciples that Jesus operates from divine knowledge.
20: ὁ λαμβάνων ἄν τινα πέμψω ἐμὲ λαμβάνει — "whoever receives anyone I send receives me," present participle of lambanō. The chain of reception (agent → sender → Father) mirrors the chain of revelation in John's theology: receiving the apostle is receiving Jesus; receiving Jesus is receiving the Father. The commissioning authority flows unbroken from the Father through Jesus to the sent ones.
21:
ἐταράχθη τῷ πνεύματι — "was troubled in spirit," aorist passive of tarassō (G5015) + dative pneumati. This is the same disturbance as at John 11:33 (the tomb) and echoes John 12:27 (the hour of death). The localization "in spirit" (pneumati) distinguishes this from the purely emotional; it is disturbance at the deepest level of Jesus' being.

ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι εἷς ἐξ ὑμῶν παραδώσει με — "truly truly I tell you, one of you will betray me," future of paradidōmi. The doubled-amen formula marks solemn revelation; the betrayal, already anticipated from 6:71 and John 12:4, is now formally announced at table.
22:
ἔβλεπον εἰς ἀλλήλους οἱ μαθηταί — "the disciples were looking at one another," imperfect of blepō — mutual, repeated glancing. The imperfect captures the prolonged confused exchange as each searches the others' faces.

ἀπορούμενοι περὶ τίνος λέγει — "at a loss about whom he was speaking," present participle of aporeō (G639, to be at a loss, have no way out). The disciples' uncertainty is genuine — Judas's hidden intention has not been visible to them.
23:
εἷς ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ ἐν τῷ κόλπῳ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ — "one of his disciples in the bosom of Jesus," kolpos (G2859, bosom/chest/lap) — the position of intimate reclining at a banquet, next to the host. The same word is used for Jesus' relationship with the Father at John 1:18 ("the one in the bosom of the Father"). The beloved disciple's position mirrors Jesus' own position with the Father.

ὃν ἠγάπα ὁ Ἰησοῦς — "whom Jesus loved," imperfect of agapaō. The imperfect marks sustained, ongoing love, not a single act. This is the first explicit designation of "the disciple whom Jesus loved" — the anonymous witness whose testimony underlies the Gospel (John 21:24).
24: νεύει οὖν τούτῳ Σίμων Πέτρος — "Simon Peter therefore nods to this one," present of neuō (G3506, to nod or beckon). A silent gesture across the reclining group — Peter cannot ask directly without disrupting the meal, so he signals the beloved disciple who has better access to Jesus.
25: ἐπιπεσὼν δὲ ἐκεῖνος οὕτως ἐπὶ τὸ στῆθος τοῦ Ἰησοῦ — "leaning back in that way upon the breast of Jesus," aorist participle of epipiptō (G1968, to fall upon/lean against). The reclining posture makes the question physically intimate; the beloved disciple simply leans back to whisper.
26:
ἐκεῖνός ἐστιν ᾧ ἐγὼ βάψω τὸ ψωμίον — "that one is the one to whom I will dip the piece of bread," future of baptō (G911, to dip). Psōmion (G5596, a morsel or small piece) is a piece of bread dipped in sauce or the dish. Offering a dipped morsel was an act of honor at a Middle Eastern meal — the host singles out a guest for distinction.

δοὺς τῷ Ἰούδᾳ Σίμωνος Ἰσκαριώτου — "having given it to Judas son of Simon Iscariot," aorist participle. The identification by name and patronymic at the moment of the morsel is the Evangelist's literary climax of the scene.
27:
εἰσῆλθεν εἰς ἐκεῖνον ὁ σατανᾶς — "Satan entered into that one," aorist of eiserchomai. Satanas (G4567) here compared to diabolos in John 13:2 — both terms are used for the adversary; the shift may indicate an escalation from prompting (John 13:2) to full possession/control. The entrance is the counterpart to the morsel: an act of honor becomes the vehicle of darkness.

ὃ ποιεῖς ποίησον τάχιον — "what you are doing, do more quickly," present + aorist imperative of poieō. Tachion (G5032, comparative of tachy, quickly) — Jesus is not hastening the betrayal for pragmatic reasons but releasing Judas to his chosen course. The authority of the command underlines that even this falls within Jesus' sovereignty.
28: τοῦτο δὲ οὐδεὶς ἔγνω τῶν ἀνακειμένων πρὸς τί εἶπεν αὐτῷ — "but no one of those reclining knew for what reason he said this to him," aorist of ginōskō. The narrator's aside emphasizes the opacity — the exchange between Jesus and Judas was perceived only by the beloved disciple (John 13:26) and perhaps Peter; the rest remain uninformed.
29: τινὲς γὰρ ἐδόκουν ἐπεὶ τὸ γλωσσόκομον εἶχεν Ἰούδας — "for some thought, since Judas had the money bag," imperfect of dokeō (G1380, to think/suppose). The narrator records the plausible misreading — Judas' role as treasurer made the most natural interpretation one of errand-running, not betrayal. The irony is that the disciples' charity toward Judas was misplaced.
30:
εὐθέως οὖν ἐξῆλθεν — "immediately therefore he went out," aorist of exerchomai. The adverb eutheōs (G2112, immediately) marks the speed of response — the decision is irrevocable, the course set.

ἦν δὲ νύξ — "and it was night," nyx (G3571). Two words in Greek, but among the most theologically dense in the Gospel. Nyx is not only a temporal marker but the Johannine symbol for spiritual darkness, opposition to the light (John 1:5; John 3:19; John 11:10). Judas goes out into the night in every sense.
31: νῦν ἐδοξάσθη ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου — "now the Son of Man was glorified," aorist passive of doxazō. The aorist is proleptic — the glorification has not yet occurred, but with Judas' departure the cross is now inevitable and imminent; the aorist treats the outcome as already secured. Nun (now) ties the glorification to this very moment of the betrayal's activation.
32:
εἰ ὁ θεὸς ἐδοξάσθη ἐν αὐτῷ, καὶ ὁ θεὸς δοξάσει αὐτὸν ἐν αὐτῷ — "if God was glorified in him, God will also glorify him in himself." The conditional is not uncertain but logical: because the Father has been glorified in the Son's mission, the Father will reciprocally glorify the Son. The mutual glorification is the structure of John's theology of the cross.

καὶ εὐθὺς δοξάσει αὐτόν — "and will glorify him at once," eutheōs (G2112). The immediacy of the coming glorification matches the immediacy of Judas' exit — the timetable is now compressed.
33:
τεκνία — "little children," diminutive of teknon (G5040). This is the only occurrence of teknia in John's Gospel (though common in 1 John). The term of endearment marks the shift in tone from instruction to farewell.

ζητήσετέ με, καὶ καθὼς εἶπον τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ὅτι ὅπου ὑπάγω ἐγὼ ὑμεῖς οὐ δύνασθε ἐλθεῖν — "you will seek me, and as I told the Jews that where I am going you cannot come." The language of departure echoes John 7:33–34 and John 8:21, but with different pathos — what was said as warning to opponents is now said as sorrowful farewell to the beloved disciples.
34:
ἐντολὴν καινὴν δίδωμι ὑμῖν — "a new commandment I give you," present of didōmi. Kainos (G2537, new in kind/quality, as opposed to neos, new in time) signals that the commandment is not merely added to existing law but is of a different order. The love commandment is not unprecedented (Leviticus 19:18) — what makes it "new" is the standard given in John 13:34b.

ἀγαπᾶτε ἀλλήλους, καθὼς ἠγάπησα ὑμᾶς — "love one another, as I have loved you," present imperative + aorist of agapaō. The kathōs (just as) makes Christ's own love the measure and model — not the natural impulse to love those who love you, but a love shaped by the self-giving pattern of John 13:1–17 and the cross. The aorist ēgapēsa encapsulates the completed act now being described as the standard.
35:
ἐν τούτῳ γνώσονται πάντες ὅτι ἐμοὶ μαθηταί ἐστε — "by this all will know that you are my disciples," future of ginōskō. The criterion of recognizable discipleship is not adherence to teaching, observance of ritual, or organizational affiliation — it is mutual love.

ἐὰν ἀγάπην ἔχητε ἐν ἀλλήλοις — "if you have love among one another," agapē (G26, disp=3). The disputed semantic range of agapē — from "warm affection" to "selfless covenantal love" — is resolved by context: the standard in John 13:34 (kathōs ēgapēsa hymas) demands a love shaped by Christ's own self-giving, which is not adequately described as mere affection. The observable social reality of this love becomes the community's public witness.
36:
κύριε, ποῦ ὑπάγεις — "Lord, where are you going?" Peter's question interrupts the farewell; hypagō (G5217, to go/depart) is the departure verb John uses for Jesus' movement toward death and to the Father (cf. John 7:33; John 8:14, 21–22; John 13:33).

οὐ δύνασαί μοι νῦν ἀκολουθῆσαι, ἀκολουθήσεις δὲ ὕστερον — "you are not able to follow me now, but you will follow later," present of dynamai + future of akoloutheō. The hysteron (later) gestures toward Peter's martyrdom (John 21:18–19), where he will indeed follow Jesus in the way of the cross.
37:
κύριε, διὰ τί οὐ δύναμαί σοι ἀκολουθεῖν ἄρτι — "Lord, why am I not able to follow you now?" Peter's arti (now) echoes Jesus' arti in John 13:7 (you do not understand now) — Peter still does not grasp the nature of what following Jesus will require.

τὴν ψυχήν μου ὑπὲρ σοῦ θήσω — "I will lay down my life for you," future of tithēmi, the same verb used for Jesus' own life-laying in John 10:11, 15. Peter claims to be ready for what is in fact Jesus' unique role — the confidence is sincere but premature.
38:
τὴν ψυχήν σου ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ θήσεις — "you will lay down your life for me?" The echo of Peter's own words (John 13:37) turned into a question — Jesus neither affirms the claim nor rejects it as impossible (Peter will eventually die for Christ), but punctures the immediate confidence.

ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω σοι, οὐ μὴ ἀλέκτωρ φωνήσῃ ἕως οὗ ἀρνήσῃ με τρίς — "truly truly I tell you, the rooster will not crow until you have denied me three times," double aorist subjunctives with the strong negation ou mē. Alektōr (G220, rooster) appears only in denial-prophecy passages in all four Gospels. The tripling of the denial (tris) will be fulfilled in John 18:15–27 with precision.
